county_metadata.record_losses: 1865 Civil War guerrilla activity damaged courthouse; some records were later recovered
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Newton County formed 1842 from Carroll County. County seat is Jasper. Located in the most rugged part of the Ozarks, including the Buffalo National River. The 1865 Civil War destruction destroyed courthouse records. Arkansas's most isolated county, strongly Unionist.
county_metadata.research_tips: Newton County's courthouse was destroyed during Civil War guerrilla activity in 1865, and records were lost. The County Clerk's office in Jasper holds post-war records. This remote Ozark county was Arkansas's most isolated, with the Buffalo River (now a National River) running through it. Newton County was strongly Unionist -- the only Confederate county officer was reportedly hanged by Union sympathizers. The Newton County Historical Society maintains reconstructed records. The county's extreme isolation means families were self-sufficient and poorly documented in official records. Church and cemetery records are critical. FamilySearch has post-war county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Newton County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Jasper holds deed books from 1842

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Ouachita County formed 1842 from Union County. County seat is Camden, on the Ouachita River. Camden was a significant antebellum river port. Site of the Battle of Poison Spring (1864).
county_metadata.research_tips: Ouachita County records at the County Clerk's office in Camden are well-preserved from 1842. Camden, on the Ouachita River, was a significant antebellum river port and cotton center. The Battle of Poison Spring (1864) occurred in Ouachita County -- one of the most controversial Civil War engagements in Arkansas involving the massacre of African American Union soldiers (1st Kansas Colored Infantry). Military records and pension files are important for African American research. The Ouachita County Historical Society maintains research files. The Camden-Ouachita County Library has genealogy collections. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Ouachita County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Camden holds deed books from 1842

source_profiles.survival_notes: Deed books complete from 1842; housed at County Clerk's office in Camden

county_metadata.record_losses: 1882 courthouse fire destroyed most early records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Perry County formed 1840 from Conway County. County seat is Perryville. Small, rural county in the Arkansas River hills. The 1882 courthouse fire destroyed most early records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Perry County's 1882 courthouse fire destroyed most early records. The County Clerk's office in Perryville holds post-fire records. This small, rural county in the Arkansas River hills was sparsely populated. The Heifer International headquarters (near Perryville) occupies the former Heifer Ranch, which has some local historical documentation. The Perry County Historical Society maintains research files. For pre-1882 records, check parent county (Conway) and the Arkansas History Commission. GLO land patents help reconstruct early land ownership. FamilySearch has microfilmed post-fire county records. Small church and cemetery records are essential.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Perry County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Perryville holds deed books from 1850

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Phillips County formed 1820 from Arkansas County. County seat is Helena-West Helena (cities merged 2006). Helena was one of the most important antebellum Mississippi River ports in Arkansas. Site of the Battle of Helena (1863). Had one of the largest enslaved populations.
county_metadata.research_tips: Phillips County records at the County Clerk's office in Helena-West Helena are well-preserved from 1820. Helena, on the Mississippi River, was one of the most important antebellum river ports in Arkansas and had the state's wealthiest planter class. The county had one of Arkansas's largest enslaved populations. The Delta Cultural Center in Helena-West Helena documents the region's African American heritage. The Battle of Helena (1863) was a significant Civil War engagement. Freedmen's Bureau records are extensive. The Phillips County Historical Museum has local materials. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm of Phillips County records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Phillips County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Helena-West Helena holds deed books from 1820

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Pike County formed 1833 from Clark and Hempstead counties. County seat is Murfreesboro. Home to Crater of Diamonds State Park, the only public diamond mine in the United States.
county_metadata.research_tips: Pike County records at the County Clerk's office in Murfreesboro are complete from 1833. Crater of Diamonds State Park (the only public diamond mine in the US) is in Pike County. The county was historically a farming and timber area in the Ouachita Mountains foothills. The Pike County Archives and History Society maintains local records and research files. Murfreesboro, the county seat, was named after the Tennessee city. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. GLO land patents document original federal land entries. Church records from Baptist and Methodist congregations are important supplemental sources.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Pike County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Murfreesboro holds deed books from 1833

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Poinsett County formed 1838 from Greene and St. Francis counties. County seat is Harrisburg. Located in northeastern Arkansas in the New Madrid earthquake zone. Named for Joel Roberts Poinsett.
county_metadata.research_tips: Poinsett County records at the County Clerk's office in Harrisburg are complete from 1838. This northeastern Arkansas delta county was part of the New Madrid earthquake zone (1811-1812), which significantly altered the landscape before organized settlement. Marked Tree, in Poinsett County, was named for a blazed tree at the trail crossing. The county was a cotton and rice farming area. The Poinsett County Historical Society maintains research files. Freedmen's Bureau records at NARA are available for African American families. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Check Greene and St. Francis county records for families predating Poinsett County.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Poinsett County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Harrisburg holds deed books from 1838

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Polk County formed 1844 from Sevier County. County seat is Mena. Located in the Ouachita Mountains of western Arkansas. Mena was a railroad town built by the Kansas City, Pittsburg and Gulf Railway.
county_metadata.research_tips: Polk County records at the County Clerk's office in Mena are complete from 1844. This Ouachita Mountains county in western Arkansas was a timber area. Mena was a railroad town built by the Kansas City, Pittsburg and Gulf Railway (later Kansas City Southern) in the 1890s, and railroad company records document many families. The Polk County Historical Museum in Mena has local materials. The Ouachita National Forest covers much of the county, and NARA holds forest reserve records. Rich Mountain, one of the highest points in Arkansas, is in Polk County. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records.

county_metadata.record_losses: 1860 and 1887 fires destroyed courthouse records; Civil War losses also significant
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Pope County formed 1829 from Crawford County. County seat is Russellville. Located in the Arkansas River valley. Fires in 1860 and 1887 and Civil War losses destroyed courthouse records. Home to Arkansas Tech University.
county_metadata.research_tips: Pope County suffered fires in 1860 and 1887 that destroyed courthouse records, along with Civil War losses. The County Clerk's office in Russellville holds surviving records. Russellville, on the Arkansas River, is home to Arkansas Tech University, whose archives hold regional materials. The county spans the Arkansas River valley with the Ozarks to the north. Nuclear One power plant (1974) brought new families documented in modern records. The Pope County Historical Foundation maintains reconstructed records. For early families, check parent county (Crawford) records and GLO land patents. FamilySearch has microfilmed surviving county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Pope County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Russellville holds deed books from 1839

county_metadata.record_losses: 1886 fire destroyed most records; Des Arc courthouse lost county records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Prairie County formed 1846 from Monroe and Pulaski counties. Has two county seats: Des Arc and DeValls Bluff. The 1886 fire destroyed most records. Located in the Grand Prairie region, a major rice-growing area.
county_metadata.research_tips: Prairie County's 1886 fire destroyed most records at the Des Arc courthouse. The county has had two county seats: Des Arc and DeValls Bluff. The County Clerk's office holds post-fire records. This central Arkansas county in the Grand Prairie region became a major rice-growing area. The Grand Prairie Historical Museum in Stuttgart (adjacent county) has regional materials. The Prairie County Historical Society maintains research files. DeValls Bluff, on the White River, was a significant Civil War supply point. For pre-1886 records, check parent county records (Monroe, Pulaski) and the Arkansas History Commission. FamilySearch has post-fire county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Prairie County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Des Arc holds deed books from 1856

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Pulaski County formed 1818 from Arkansas County as one of the original territorial counties. County seat is Little Rock, the state capital. The most populous county in Arkansas. Home to the Arkansas History Commission (State Archives), the Old State House, and numerous research institutions.
county_metadata.research_tips: Pulaski County records at the County Clerk's office in Little Rock are extensive and well-preserved from 1818. Little Rock is the state capital and the seat of Pulaski County, making it the center for both state and county records. The Arkansas History Commission (State Archives) in Little Rock is the primary state repository. The Central Arkansas Library System's Butler Center for Arkansas Studies is one of the finest genealogical collections in the state. The Historic Arkansas Museum and the Old State House Museum hold historical materials. Pulaski County was a parent county for many others, so early settlers may appear in Pulaski records. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm. Little Rock city directories from the 1870s are available.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Pulaski County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Little Rock holds deed books from 1818

source_profiles.survival_notes: Deed books complete from 1818; housed at County Clerk's office in Little Rock
